problems.name: Problem 1 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: What is the smallest sum of two $3$-digit numbers that can be obtained by placing each of the six digits $4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9$ in one of the six boxes in this addition problem? [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 2 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Which digit of $0.12345$, when changed to $9$, gives the largest number?

problems.statement: What fraction of the square is shaded? [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 4 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Which of the following could not be the unit's digit [one's digit] of the square of a whole number?

problems.name: Problem 5 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Which of the following is closest to the product $(0.48017) (0.48017)(0.48017)$?

problems.name: Problem 6 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Which of these five numbers is the largest?

problems.name: Problem 8 (1990 AJHSME)

problems.name: Problem 9 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: The grading scale shown is used at Jones Junior High: A: $93-100$, B: $85-92$, C: $75-84$, D: $70-74$, F: $0-69$. The fifteen scores in Mr. Freeman's class were: $89, 72, 54, 97, 77, 92, 85, 74, 75, 63, 84, 78, 71, 80, 90$. In Mr. Freeman's class, what percent of the students received a grade of C?

problems.name: Problem 10 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: On this monthly calendar, the date behind one of the letters is added to the date behind $C$. If this sum equals the sum of the dates behind $A$ and $B$, then the letter is [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 11 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: The numbers on the faces of this cube are consecutive whole numbers. The sums of the two numbers on each of the three pairs of opposite faces are equal. The sum of the six numbers on this cube is [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 12 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: There are twenty-four $4$-digit numbers that use each of the four digits $2, 4, 5,$ and $7$ exactly once. Listed in numerical order from smallest to largest, the number in the $17$th position in the list is

problems.name: Problem 13 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: One proposal for new postage rates for a letter was $30$ cents for the first ounce and $22$ cents for each additional ounce (or fraction of an ounce). The postage for a letter weighing $4.5$ ounces was

problems.name: Problem 14 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: A bag contains only blue balls and green balls. There are $6$ blue balls. If the probability of drawing a blue ball at random from this bag is $\frac{1}{4}$, then the number of green balls in the bag is

problems.name: Problem 15 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: The area of this figure is $100\text{ cm}^2$. Its perimeter is [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 17 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: A straight concrete sidewalk is to be $3$ feet wide, $60$ feet long, and $3$ inches thick. How many cubic yards of concrete must a contractor order for the sidewalk if concrete must be ordered in a whole number of cubic yards?

problems.name: Problem 18 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Each corner of a rectangular prism is cut off. Two (of the eight) cuts are shown. Assume that the planes cutting the prism do not intersect anywhere in or on the prism. How many edges does the new figure have? [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 19 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: There are $120$ seats in a row. What is the fewest number of seats that must be occupied so the next person to be seated must sit next to someone?

problems.name: Problem 20 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: The annual incomes of $1000$ families range from $8200$ dollars to $98000$ dollars. In error, the largest income was entered on the computer as $980000$ dollars. The difference between the mean of the incorrect data and the mean of the actual data is

problems.name: Problem 21 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: A list of $8$ numbers is formed by beginning with two given numbers. Each new number in the list is the product of the two previous numbers. Find the first number if the last three are shown: $\dots, \dots, \dots, \dots, \dots, 16, 64, 1024$

problems.name: Problem 22 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Several students are seated at a large circular table. They pass around a bag containing $100$ pieces of candy. Each person receives the bag, takes one piece of candy and then passes the bag to the next person. If Chris takes the first and last piece of candy, then the number of students at the table could be

problems.name: Problem 23 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: The graph relates the distance traveled (in miles) to the time elapsed (in hours) on a trip taken by an experimental airplane. During which hour was the average speed of this airplane the largest? [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 24 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: Three $\Delta$'s and a $\bigcirc$ will balance nine $\square$'s. One $\Delta$ will balance a $\bigcirc$ and a $\square$. How many $\square$'s will balance the two $\bigcirc$'s in this balance? [See figure in original PDF]

problems.name: Problem 25 (1990 AJHSME)
problems.statement: How many different patterns can be made by shading exactly two of the nine squares? Patterns that can be matched by flips and/or turns are not considered different. For example, the patterns shown below are not considered different. [See figure in original PDF]
